title: AI vs ML vs Deep Learning (in one mental model)
summary: A simple mental model + examples to explain AI, ML, and Deep Learning clearly in interviews and in your projects.
hook: Most people mix up AI, ML, and Deep Learning — and it shows in interviews.
body: AI is the umbrella: any system that shows “intelligent” behavior (rules, search, planning, ML).
ML is a subset of AI: models learn patterns from data instead of hand-written rules.
Deep Learning is a subset of ML: neural nets learn representations (often end-to-end) from lots of data/compute.
Use-case map: Rules (AI) → fraud checks; ML → churn prediction; DL → vision/LLMs/speech.
Interview tip: always mention data, objective, and evaluation metric (accuracy/F1/AUC, etc.).
cta: If you’re starting AI/ML, comment “ROADMAP” and I’ll share a beginner path I follow.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[AI: umbrella] --> B[Rules / Search / Planning]
  A --> C[Machine Learning]
  C --> D[Deep Learning]
  B --> B1[If-else rules]
  B --> B2[Graph search]
  C --> C1[Regression/Classification]
  C --> C2[Clustering]
  D --> D1[CNNs]
  D --> D2[Transformers/LLMs]

title: Supervised vs Unsupervised vs Reinforcement Learning
summary: How to choose the learning paradigm and explain it with crisp real-world examples.
hook: Choosing the right ML paradigm is 80% of the solution clarity.
body: Supervised learning: labeled data (x → y). Example: spam detection, price prediction.
Unsupervised learning: unlabeled data. Example: customer segmentation, anomaly discovery.
Reinforcement learning: agent learns by rewards in an environment. Example: robotics, games, dynamic pricing.
Rule of thumb: if you can define a target label reliably → supervised; if you need structure discovery → unsupervised; if actions affect future states → RL.
Always validate with data constraints: labels cost, drift, feedback loops, and latency requirements.
cta: Which one are you working on right now: supervised, unsupervised, or RL?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  Q{Do you have labels?} -->|Yes| S[Supervised]
  Q -->|No| U{Are actions involved?}
  U -->|No| US[Unsupervised]
  U -->|Yes| RL[Reinforcement Learning]
  S --> S1[Classification / Regression]
  US --> US1[Clustering / Anomaly]
  RL --> RL1[Policy learns from rewards]

title: Overfitting vs Underfitting (and how to fix each)
summary: A practical checklist for diagnosing model performance gaps and applying the right fix.
hook: If your train score is great but test score is bad, your model isn’t “smart” — it’s memorizing.
body: Underfitting = model too simple or not trained enough: both train and test performance are poor.
Overfitting = model too complex or data too small/noisy: train performance high, test performance drops.
Fix underfitting: add features, increase model capacity, train longer, reduce regularization.
Fix overfitting: more data, stronger regularization, simpler model, early stopping, data augmentation, better validation splits.
Best habit: track learning curves (train vs validation) before changing algorithms.
cta: Want a learning-curve template in Python? Comment “CURVE”.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Start] --> B[Check train vs val]
  B -->|Train low, Val low| U[Underfitting]
  B -->|Train high, Val low| O[Overfitting]
  U --> U1[More features]
  U --> U2[More capacity / train longer]
  O --> O1[More data]
  O --> O2[Regularization / early stop]
  O --> O3[Simpler model]

title: Confusion Matrix: the 5-minute interview answer
summary: Turn accuracy into a real evaluation story using precision, recall, F1, and trade-offs.
hook: Accuracy is a trap when classes are imbalanced.
body: Confusion matrix gives you TP, FP, FN, TN — the raw story behind a classifier.
Precision = TP/(TP+FP): how many predicted positives were correct.
Recall = TP/(TP+FN): how many actual positives you captured.
F1 balances precision and recall; use it when both matter.
Business framing: fraud detection often cares about recall (catch fraud), but too many FP hurts user experience.
cta: If you want, I can share a cheat-sheet for metrics by use-case.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[Predicted Positive] --> B[TP]
  A --> C[FP]
  D[Predicted Negative] --> E[TN]
  D --> F[FN]
  B --> P[Precision = TP/(TP+FP)]
  B --> R[Recall = TP/(TP+FN)]

title: Data leakage: the silent killer of ML projects
summary: What leakage looks like, why it happens, and how to prevent it with correct splitting and pipelines.
hook: A model with 99% accuracy can still be useless in production.
body: Data leakage happens when training sees information it wouldn’t have at prediction time.
Common leakage: using future information, global scaling before split, target-encoded features without CV, duplicates across splits.
Fix: split first, then fit preprocessing only on training data (pipelines).
For time series: use time-based splits (no shuffling).
Always sanity-check with a “leakage baseline”: can you predict the label using obviously-cheating features?
cta: If you’ve faced leakage before, what caused it? I’m collecting real examples.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Raw data] --> B{Split?}
  B -->|Wrong| W[Preprocess on full data]
  W --> WL[Leakage → inflated score]
  B -->|Right| T[Train split]
  B --> V[Val/Test split]
  T --> P1[Fit preprocess]
  P1 --> M[Train model]
  P1 --> P2[Transform val/test]
  P2 --> E[Evaluate]

title: Python data pipeline for ML: a clean structure
summary: A practical way to structure notebooks vs scripts so your ML work becomes reusable and production-ready.
hook: If your ML project lives only in a notebook, scaling it will hurt.
body: Keep exploration in notebooks, but move stable logic to modules (data loading, features, training).
Use config-driven runs: dataset path, seed, model params, output directory.
Persist artifacts: model, metrics JSON, plots, and data version references.
Make one function the entry point: train(config) → returns model + metrics.
You’ll thank yourself when you do ablations, CV, and deployment.
cta: Want a minimal repo template? I can share a structure I use for ML projects.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  N[Notebook: explore] --> S[src/: reusable code]
  S --> D[data.py]
  S --> F[features.py]
  S --> T[train.py]
  T --> A[artifacts/]
  A --> M[model.pkl]
  A --> R[metrics.json]
  A --> P[plots/]

title: NumPy mental models that make you faster
summary: Broadcasting, vectorization, and shapes — the 3 things that unlock speed and clarity.
hook: Most NumPy bugs are shape bugs.
body: Always think in shapes: (n,), (n,1), (n,d). Write them down while coding.
Vectorization beats Python loops: use matrix ops, boolean masks, and aggregation functions.
Broadcasting: dimensions of size 1 can expand to match the other operand — powerful but easy to misuse.
Use .reshape and keepdims=True intentionally to avoid accidental broadcasting.
Debug tip: print array.shape and use small toy arrays to validate logic.
cta: If you want, I’ll post a mini cheatsheet: reshape vs ravel vs squeeze.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Define shapes] --> B[Choose vectorized ops]
  B --> C[Check broadcasting]
  C --> D[Run on toy data]
  D --> E[Scale to full data]

title: R (tidyverse) for ML data work: 6 patterns that save hours
summary: Core tidyverse patterns for cleaning + feature prep: joins, group_by, NA handling, and reproducible pipelines.
hook: R is still one of the fastest ways to go from raw data → clean features (especially with tidyverse).
body: Joins with dplyr: left_join/inner_join and always check row counts after merges.
group_by + summarise: build features like counts, means, and time-based aggregates.
Missing values: use explicit NA logic (and consider missing-indicator features).
Dates: parse early (lubridate) and derive week/month/holiday-style features.
Categoricals: use factor levels intentionally to keep training/inference consistent.
Repro pipelines: keep data steps in one script (or targets/drake) instead of scattered cells.
cta: If you want, I’ll share an R checklist I use before training any model (audit + leakage checks).
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Raw tables] --> B[Merge]
  B --> C[Audit row counts]
  C --> D[Handle missing]
  D --> E[Feature engineering]
  E --> F[Train-ready dataset]

title: Train/val/test split: what I do in real projects
summary: A practical guide to splitting data (random, group, time) and keeping evaluation honest.
hook: Bad splitting can make a bad model look amazing.
body: Random split is fine when data points are i.i.d (no grouping, no time order).
Group split when the same entity appears multiple times (users, devices, patients).
Time split for sequences (transactions, sensor logs): always predict the future from the past.
Keep a true holdout test set for final reporting (don’t tune on it).
Use seeds for reproducibility and log the exact split strategy.
cta: What kind of data do you work with most: i.i.d, grouped, or time-series?
image_bullets: Random vs Group vs Time split
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Dataset] --> B{Any groups?}
  B -->|Yes| G[GroupSplit]
  B -->|No| C{Time order matters?}
  C -->|Yes| T[TimeSplit]
  C -->|No| R[RandomSplit]
  G --> H[Holdout test]
  T --> H
  R --> H

title: scikit-learn Pipelines: the best habit you can build
summary: Pipelines prevent leakage, improve reproducibility, and make ML code deployable.
hook: If you preprocess outside a Pipeline, you’re one step away from leakage.
body: Pipeline bundles preprocessing + model into one object: fit on train, transform everywhere.
It makes cross-validation correct by construction (each fold fits transforms separately).
You can persist the entire pipeline and serve it in production reliably.
Pair it with ColumnTransformer for mixed numeric/categorical features.
Bonus: GridSearchCV becomes cleaner and less error-prone.
cta: If you want, I’ll share a minimal Pipeline example (numeric + categorical + model).
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[Train data] --> B[fit preprocess]
  B --> C[train model]
  A2[New data] --> D[transform (same preprocess)]
  D --> E[predict]
  B --> D

title: RAG in one sentence (and why it works)
summary: RAG = retrieval + generation. Here’s the intuition and when it beats fine-tuning.
hook: RAG is how you make LLMs answer from YOUR documents.
body: RAG retrieves relevant context (documents/chunks) and feeds it to the LLM before generation.
It reduces hallucination by grounding answers in sources.
It’s cheaper and faster to update than fine-tuning: update the index, not the model.
RAG shines when knowledge changes frequently or must be auditable (citations).
Key idea: quality = retrieval quality + prompt quality + model capability.
cta: If you want, I’ll share a simple RAG architecture diagram and checklist.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  U[User question] --> R[Retriever]
  R --> V[Vector DB]
  V --> C[Top-k chunks]
  C --> P[Prompt builder]
  P --> L[LLM]
  L --> A[Answer + sources]

title: Chunking strategy: the underrated part of RAG
summary: Chunk size, overlap, and structure choices that directly change retrieval quality.
hook: Bad chunking creates bad retrieval, even with the best embedding model.
body: Chunk by meaning, not by fixed characters: headings, sections, bullet lists, tables.
Start with 300–800 tokens per chunk and 10–20% overlap (then tune).
Keep metadata: source URL, title, section heading, date, and permissions.
For code/docs: chunk by function/class boundaries; for policies: chunk by clause.
Measure retrieval: hit-rate@k, MRR, and user success metrics (not just “looks good”).
cta: If you share your doc type (PDFs, web pages, code), I can suggest chunking rules.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  D[Documents] --> S[Structure-aware splitter]
  S --> K[Chunks + metadata]
  K --> E[Embed]
  E --> I[Index into Vector DB]

title: Embeddings: what they are and how to think about them
summary: Embeddings map text into vectors so “similar meaning” becomes “nearby in space”.
hook: RAG is basically: ‘search by meaning’ + ‘answer with citations’.
body: An embedding converts text into a dense vector (e.g., 768 dimensions).
Semantic similarity is often measured with cosine similarity (angle between vectors).
Good embeddings capture intent and context: “refund policy” ~ “return and reimbursement”.
Indexing needs trade-offs: recall vs latency vs cost (vector DB config matters).
If retrieval is off, evaluate embeddings + chunking + query rewriting.
cta: Want an embeddings + cosine similarity explanation with a tiny numeric example?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart LR
  Q[Query] --> EQ[Embed query]
  D[Chunks] --> ED[Embed chunks]
  EQ --> S[Similarity search]
  ED --> S
  S --> K[Top-k chunks]

title: RAG evaluation: what to measure (beyond vibes)
summary: A practical evaluation stack for RAG: retrieval metrics, answer metrics, and end-user outcomes.
hook: If you can’t measure RAG quality, you can’t improve it.
body: Retrieval metrics: hit-rate@k, MRR, and relevance judgment (human or weak labels).
Answer metrics: groundedness (uses retrieved sources), faithfulness, and completeness.
Latency + cost: measure tokens, vector search time, and total response time.
Test sets: build 50–200 real user questions with expected sources (start small).
Iterate systematically: chunking → embeddings → re-ranking → prompting → guardrails.
cta: If you want, I’ll share a simple RAG eval spreadsheet format.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[User questions set] --> B[Run RAG]
  B --> C[Log retrieval + answer]
  C --> D[Retrieval metrics]
  C --> E[Answer metrics]
  C --> F[Latency/cost]
  D --> G[Improve components]
  E --> G
  F --> G

title: Re-ranking: the easiest RAG upgrade many skip
summary: A 2-stage retrieval pipeline (retrieve → re-rank) can dramatically improve relevance.
hook: Vector search gets you candidates. Re-ranking gets you the best answer context.
body: Stage 1: fast retriever gets top-50 candidates (cheap).
Stage 2: re-ranker scores candidates with a stronger model (cross-encoder / LLM) and selects top-5.
This reduces ‘nearby but wrong’ chunks (common in dense retrieval).
It’s often a better ROI than switching embedding models.
Evaluate before/after with hit-rate@k and user satisfaction.
cta: If you’re building RAG, are you using re-ranking yet?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  Q[Query] --> R1[Retriever]
  R1 --> C[Top-50 candidates]
  C --> RR[Re-ranker]
  RR --> K[Top-5 context]
  K --> L[LLM answer]

title: What is an AI agent (without the hype)?
summary: An agent = LLM + tools + memory + a loop that plans and acts toward a goal.
hook: An LLM answers. An agent acts.
body: Agent core: goal → plan → tool calls → observe results → refine → finish.
Tools can be APIs, databases, search, code execution, or internal services.
Memory helps maintain context: short-term (conversation) and long-term (user/project facts).
Guardrails matter: tool permissions, safe browsing, and human-in-the-loop for risky actions.
Best starting use-cases: repetitive ops tasks, report generation, customer support triage.
cta: If you want, I’ll share a minimal agent design pattern you can implement in Python.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  G[Goal] --> P[Plan]
  P --> T[Call tool]
  T --> O[Observe]
  O --> R{Done?}
  R -->|No| P
  R -->|Yes| F[Final output]

title: Tool calling: the ‘API layer’ of agents
summary: How agents reliably use tools: schemas, retries, validation, and logging.
hook: Agents fail in the same way software fails: bad inputs and missing validation.
body: Define tool schemas clearly: required fields, types, and constraints.
Validate tool outputs: don’t trust external APIs blindly; check structure and errors.
Add retries + backoff for transient failures; make tools idempotent where possible.
Log everything: prompt, tool call, tool response, final answer (with redaction for secrets).
Design for fallbacks: if tool fails, return partial results + next steps.
cta: If you’re building agents, what tool failures do you see most often?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[LLM decides tool call] --> V[Validate inputs]
  V --> C[Call tool]
  C --> O{Tool ok?}
  O -->|No| R[Retry/backoff]
  R --> C
  O -->|Yes| P[Post-validate output]
  P --> F[Compose final answer]

title: AI automation checklist before you ship
summary: A pragmatic checklist: reliability, safety, monitoring, and ROI.
hook: Automation isn’t ‘cool’. It must be reliable.
body: Define success: time saved, error reduction, cost per run, and user satisfaction.
Create a fallback: when confidence is low, ask a human or route to a safer path.
Add guardrails: allowlist tools, limit permissions, and restrict external actions.
Monitor: failure rate, latency, token spend, tool error types, and drift in prompts/data.
Test with real cases: 30–100 scenarios beats 1 demo every time.
cta: If you want, I can convert your workflow into an automation plan with milestones.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  W[Workflow] --> M[Define metrics]
  M --> D[Design fallback]
  D --> G[Guardrails]
  G --> T[Test scenarios]
  T --> S[Ship]
  S --> O[Observe/monitor]
  O --> I[Iterate]

title: Memory in agents: what to store (and what not to)
summary: A simple approach to agent memory that avoids privacy risk and improves performance.
hook: Storing everything is not memory — it’s a liability.
body: Short-term memory: current task context (recent messages, intermediate results).
Long-term memory: stable user preferences, project facts, and reusable decisions.
Don’t store secrets or raw PII; store pointers/refs and redacted summaries.
Use retrieval memory: save chunks with metadata and retrieve by query (like RAG for conversations).
Expire memory: TTL and “forget” actions are product features, not afterthoughts.
cta: If you’re building agents, are you storing memory as text, vectors, or both?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  U[User] --> A[Agent]
  A --> STM[Short-term context]
  A -->|write summary| LTM[(Long-term store)]
  A -->|retrieve| LTM
  LTM --> R[Relevant memories]
  R --> A

title: Multi-agent vs single-agent: when to split
summary: How to decide between one capable agent and multiple specialized agents (and the trade-offs).
hook: More agents ≠ better system. Sometimes it’s just more failure modes.
body: Start single-agent: simpler, easier to debug, lower coordination overhead.
Split when tasks have different skills/tools (research vs coding vs execution) or different permission levels.
Add an orchestrator: routes sub-tasks, merges outputs, enforces constraints.
Watch failure modes: deadlocks, duplicated work, conflicting outputs.
Keep shared state minimal and explicit (task spec, artifacts, and acceptance checks).
cta: If you’ve tried multi-agent, what coordination problem did you hit first?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  O[Orchestrator] --> R[Research agent]
  O --> C[Coding agent]
  O --> X[Execution agent]
  R --> O
  C --> O
  X --> O
  O --> F[Final delivery]

title: Big-O: the only explanation you need to remember
summary: How to explain time/space complexity in interviews using growth rate intuition.
hook: Big-O is not about speed. It’s about how your solution scales.
body: Big-O describes the growth of runtime as input size n increases (dominant term).
Common: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2).
Rules: loops → multiply, sequential blocks → add, log n often from halving/binary search.
Always mention space complexity too (especially for recursion and hash maps).
Interview hack: start with a brute-force solution, then optimize and explain the trade-off.
cta: Want a Big-O practice sheet with 15 common patterns? Comment “BIG-O”.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Problem] --> B[Brute force]
  B --> C[Analyze Big-O]
  C --> D[Find bottleneck]
  D --> E[Optimize with pattern]
  E --> F[Explain trade-offs]

title: HashMap pattern: the fastest way to improve a solution
summary: Turn nested loops into O(n) with a HashMap — and explain it cleanly.
hook: If you see ‘find pair’, ‘count’, or ‘seen before’, think HashMap.
body: HashMap helps store seen elements / frequencies / indices for O(1) average lookup.
Classic problems: Two Sum, anagrams, longest substring without repeat, frequency counts.
Trade-off: more space to reduce time (time-space trade-off).
In interviews: describe what the map stores and when it updates.
Edge cases: duplicates, negative numbers, and initialization (0 vs 1 indexing).
cta: If you want, I’ll post a “HashMap patterns” list with example problems.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Iterate elements] --> B{Key in map?}
  B -->|Yes| C[Use stored info]
  B -->|No| D[Store/update]
  C --> E[Return/continue]
  D --> A

title: Two pointers: the pattern behind many ‘easy’ solutions
summary: How to spot two-pointer opportunities and explain the invariant.
hook: If your data is sorted (or can be sorted), two pointers might be the optimal move.
body: Use two pointers to reduce nested loops: left/right, slow/fast, window boundaries.
Common: pair sum in sorted array, remove duplicates, palindromes, container with most water.
Invariant = what remains true as pointers move (e.g., window contains unique chars).
Sorting can enable two pointers but adds O(n log n). Mention the trade-off.
Be explicit about pointer moves: why left++ vs right--.
cta: Want a ‘two pointers’ problem set with difficulty progression?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart LR
  L[Left pointer] --> M[Check condition]
  R[Right pointer] --> M
  M -->|Need smaller| R2[Move right--]
  M -->|Need bigger| L2[Move left++]
  R2 --> M
  L2 --> M

title: Sliding window: from O(n²) to O(n)
summary: A mental model for fixed and variable windows with real interview examples.
hook: If the question says ‘subarray’ or ‘substring’, sliding window is shouting.
body: Fixed window: window size k stays constant (max sum of k elements).
Variable window: expand right pointer, shrink left to satisfy constraint (unique chars, sum ≤ target).
Maintain state incrementally (counts, sum, max/min) instead of recomputing.
Be careful with constraints that break monotonicity (negative numbers can complicate).
Explain what your window state represents at every step.
cta: I can share 10 sliding window problems with ‘state’ hints if you want.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Expand right] --> B[Update state]
  B --> C{Constraint ok?}
  C -->|Yes| D[Update answer]
  C -->|No| E[Shrink left]
  E --> B
  D --> A

title: Binary search: not just for sorted arrays
summary: Binary search on answers (monotonic property) is a game-changer for medium problems.
hook: Binary search is a technique for monotonic truth — not only sorted arrays.
body: Classic: search a value in sorted array → O(log n).
Binary search on answer: find minimum/maximum feasible value using a predicate check(mid).
Works when predicate is monotonic (true then always true, or false then always false).
Examples: minimum speed to finish tasks, capacity to ship packages, allocate pages.
Interview framing: define predicate, bounds, and why monotonicity holds.
cta: Want a list of ‘binary search on answer’ problems with predicate templates?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Define bounds lo..hi] --> B[mid=(lo+hi)/2]
  B --> C{check(mid)}
  C -->|feasible| D[hi=mid]
  C -->|not feasible| E[lo=mid+1]
  D --> B
  E --> B

title: Build a mini RAG chatbot project (the clean roadmap)
summary: A practical roadmap: data → chunking → embeddings → retrieval → eval → UI.
hook: If you want one portfolio project in 2026: build a RAG app with evaluation.
body: Pick a domain: your notes, a public handbook, docs of a library, or company policies.
Ingest + clean: extract text, remove noise, keep metadata (source/section/date).
Chunking + embeddings: tune for your doc type; index in a vector DB.
Retrieval: top-k + (optional) re-ranking; then prompt the LLM with citations.
Evaluation: build a small question set and track retrieval + answer quality.
cta: If you want, I can outline a repo structure and MVP milestones for your RAG app.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  D[Docs] --> I[Ingest + clean]
  I --> S[Split into chunks]
  S --> E[Embeddings]
  E --> V[Vector DB]
  Q[Question] --> R[Retrieve + re-rank]
  V --> R
  R --> P[Prompt with citations]
  P --> L[LLM]
  L --> A[Answer]
  A --> EV[Evaluate + iterate]

title: From notebook to deploy: the MLOps ladder (simple version)
summary: A step-by-step ladder to make your ML project production-like without over-engineering.
hook: You don’t need ‘full MLOps’ on day 1 — you need the right ladder.
body: Step 1: reproducible training (seed, config, saved artifacts).
Step 2: evaluation + test set discipline, plus experiment tracking (even a CSV).
Step 3: pipeline packaging (sklearn Pipeline) and model versioning.
Step 4: simple API (FastAPI) + input validation + monitoring hooks.
Step 5: scheduled retraining only when drift or performance drop is detected.
cta: If you’re building a project now, what step are you at?
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  N[Notebook] --> R[Repro run]
  R --> E[Evaluation + tracking]
  E --> V[Version artifacts]
  V --> A[Serve via API]
  A --> M[Monitor]
  M -->|drift| T[Retrain]
  T --> V

title: How I answer ‘Tell me about your AI project’
summary: A structured interview answer that highlights impact, ownership, and technical depth.
hook: A great AI project answer is not a feature dump. It’s a story.
body: Context: what problem, why it mattered, and who used it.
Approach: data, model choice, evaluation metrics, and baseline comparisons.
Engineering: pipeline, monitoring, latency/cost constraints, and failure handling.
Impact: measurable results (time saved, accuracy lift, cost reduction).
Reflection: what you’d improve next (data, eval, product iteration).
cta: If you want, paste your project in 5 lines and I’ll help shape it into this format.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Context] --> B[Baseline]
  B --> C[Approach]
  C --> D[Evaluation]
  D --> E[Deployment]
  E --> F[Impact]
  F --> G[Next improvements]

title: Automate your workflow with an AI agent (safe MVP)
summary: A safe MVP plan: start read-only, add tools gradually, and measure ROI.
hook: The safest agent is the one that starts read-only.
body: MVP scope: summarize emails/tickets, draft replies, create task lists, generate reports.
Start with read-only tools: search, database read, docs retrieval — no write actions.
Add confidence gates: if the agent is unsure, ask clarifying questions or escalate.
Introduce write tools last (create tickets, send emails) with approval steps.
Track ROI: minutes saved per week, reduction in human errors, and user adoption.
cta: Tell me your workflow (1 paragraph) and I’ll suggest an agent MVP scope.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  W[Workflow] --> R[Read-only agent MVP]
  R --> G[Confidence gates]
  G --> H{Need approval?}
  H -->|Yes| U[Human approve]
  H -->|No| A[Auto action]
  U --> A
  A --> M[Measure ROI]

title: 30-day AI series: what to learn next (and how to stay consistent)
summary: A wrap-up roadmap for continuing AI/ML, RAG, DSA, and agent building with consistent output.
hook: Consistency is a skill — and it compounds faster than motivation.
body: Pick one track for the next 30 days: (1) ML foundations, (2) RAG systems, (3) Agents/automation, (4) DSA interviews.
Ship weekly: one mini-project or one case study post per week beats endless tutorials.
Create a feedback loop: track what posts get saves/comments and refine topics.
Build in public: share failures, trade-offs, and how you measured improvements.
Keep a simple cadence: learn → build → write → repeat.
cta: If you followed this series, comment which track you want next — I’ll continue it.
diagram_mermaid:
flowchart TD
  L[Learn] --> B[Build]
  B --> W[Write/Share]
  W --> F[Feedback]
